title: Odd pathways speed up self-assembly
authors: Dawid Dopierała, Luca Cocconi, Robert L. Jack, Anton Souslov
abstract: Active self-assembly can bypass equilibrium bottlenecks through external energy injection. However, generic driving typically distorts target structures and requires sustained energy input even after assembly is complete. Here, we investigate a class of non-reciprocal interactions that accelerates assembly while preserving the equilibrium Boltzmann distribution. The probability currents induced by these odd interactions reshape fundamental processes, including activated barrier crossing, soft-mode relaxation, and transitions between metastable states. In particular, these currents enhance Arrhenius rates by driving particles across otherwise inaccessible free-energy barriers. We show that this acceleration arises from an effective increase in the mobility of the reaction coordinate, mediated by non-reciprocal coupling between mechanical modes. In turn, we discover a trade-off between kinetic acceleration and power dissipation when active forces are engaged. Our results suggest a route to energy-efficient, high-fidelity self-assembly via active catalysts that transiently accelerate relaxation toward equilibrium targets and deactivate upon reaching the desired state.
summary: The paper investigates how 'odd' or non-reciprocal forces can be used to speed up the self-assembly of mechanical structures. It demonstrates that these forces can drive particles across energy barriers to accelerate transitions without changing the final equilibrium state. The authors identify a fundamental trade-off between the speed of assembly and the energy dissipated by the active forces. This suggests a 'catalytic' strategy where active forces are used transiently to drive assembly and then deactivated to maintain high-fidelity structures.
main_problem: How to accelerate the kinetics of self-assembly and transition between metastable states without altering the equilibrium Boltzmann distribution or distorting the final target structures.
main_result: Non-reciprocal (odd) interactions induce probability currents that accelerate assembly via mode coupling and increased effective mobility, though this comes at the cost of increased power dissipation.
method: Analytical derivation using Langevin and Fokker-Planck equations, adiabatic elimination of fast modes, and numerical simulations of stochastic dynamics.
model_or_system: Mechanical networks of overdamped thermal colloids subject to non-conservative transverse forces, including specific models like two-particle odd springs, odd triangles, and four/six-particle square networks.
key_observables: Probability currents, transition rates (flip rates), assembly time, power dissipation, and steady-state probability distributions.
important_parameters: Non-reciprocal coupling strength (ka), spring constant (k), temperature (T), and angular potential strength (epsilon).
main_assumptions: Low-temperature regime, separation of time scales between fast and slow modes, and small deformation approximations for certain analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows steady-state distributions, transition rates, and assembly trajectories; Figure 2 illustrates the path from square to line; Figure A1 displays elastic eigenmodes of the triangle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of kinetic bottlenecks, presents a framework for non-reciprocal forces, analyzes specific small-scale models (2, 3, and 4-particle systems), explores complex assembly (double-square), and concludes with a discussion on the energetic trade-offs and catalytic applications.
why_interesting: The study of non-reciprocal dynamics and the decoupling of kinetics from thermodynamics is highly relevant to open quantum systems and the control of non-equilibrium steady states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Transverse forces as effective temperature. How do transverse forces affect self-assembly? a, Abstraction of transverse forces: pairs of metallodielectric (Janus) colloids driven by a uniform AC electric field [18], Kilobots [19], and ball-and- spring model. Battery represents energy injection, which results in transverse forces (red arrows) proportional to ka(r −r0). b, Abstraction of self-assembly: thermal noise leads to the assembly of target structures, for example in colloidomers [20]. Resulting phenomenology: c, Steady-state probability distribution for two-particle system (top, inset) remains the same with and without transverse forces (top). However, transverse forces...

FIG. 2. Activity-enhanced folding. a, Steady-state probability distribution of the angles β and γ for a rhombic configuration of four springs. Horizontal and vertical lines indicate transitions via wedge states, while diagonals correspond to rhombus configurations. Zoom of the first quadrant (right) shows that, starting from the square configuration (centre of the diagonal), the system first assembles into a line with the length of two springs. In the steady state, the system is biased towards near-zero β configurations (left). (T = 0.001, ka = 0) b, Sample trajectories of the system signed area A and histograms of folding times over many runs (shaded). Transverse forces reduce the folding...

FIG. 3. Acceleration of transitions between metastable states. a, Ladder (top) and chevron (bottom) are metastable configurations of the double-square system enforced by the Morse interaction (halos indicate interaction range; faded halos denote effectively non-interacting particles).
